1: The angelic figure confirms that he also stood to support Darius the Mede in his first year. The heavenly involvement in the transition of empires is continuous — the succession from Babylon to Persia to Greece to Rome is not merely political but providentially ordered. Paul's declaration that 'there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God' (Romans 13:1) rests on this Danielic vision: the succession of empires is the outworking of divine sovereignty preparing the world for the fullness of time (Galatians 4:4).
2: Three more Persian kings and then a fourth, the mightiest, who will stir up Greece. The prophetic foreshortening of Persian history prepares for the main event: the arrival of the mighty Greek king. The historical sweep emphasizes that no empire, however powerful, is permanent; all are temporary stewards of an authority that belongs ultimately to Christ: 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matthew 28:18) — the authority that passed through these empires is finally concentrated in the risen Lord.
3: 'Then a mighty king shall arise, who shall rule with great dominion and do as he wills.' The mighty king of Greece — Alexander the Great — 'shall do as he wills' (yaʿăśeh kiṣônô): the absolute exercise of self-willed power. The contrast with Christ is pointed: Jesus does not do his own will but the Father's ('For I have come down from heaven, not to do my own will but the will of him who sent me,' John 6:38). The mighty king whose will is self-directed passes away (Daniel 11:4); the Son whose will is aligned with the Father receives an eternal kingdom (Daniel 7:14).
4: 'And as soon as he has arisen, his kingdom shall be broken and divided toward the four winds of heaven, but not to his posterity.' The brevity of Alexander's empire — the mightiest personal dominion in the ancient world shattered at his death. The contrast with Christ is sharp: Jesus establishes a kingdom 'that shall never be destroyed' (Daniel 2:44) and his 'posterity' — those born again into his family — are innumerable (Psalms 22:30; Isaiah 53:10: 'He shall see his offspring'). What Alexander could not pass on, Christ distributes as an inheritance to all who receive him (John 1:12).
5: The king of the south (Ptolemy I) grows strong; one of his princes becomes stronger still (Seleucus I). The jostling of the Diadochi — the competing heirs to Alexander's divided empire — begins the long north-south conflict that will devastate the 'beautiful land' (Israel). The covenant people are caught between empires, as the church will be caught between worldly powers in subsequent ages. Christ's promise 'I will never leave you nor forsake you' (Hebrews 13:5) is given to a community that will face precisely these competing pressures.
6: The failed alliance between north and south through political marriage — the daughter of the south given to the king of the north, but the alliance collapses. Political marriages as instruments of stability — the same system of human calculation that God's covenant transcends. Christ is not a political alliance but a covenant: 'the new covenant in my blood' (Luke 22:20) ratified by his own body, not by a diplomatic arrangement that can be broken when interests diverge.
7: A branch from her roots takes up his father's position and acts against the northern king's army. The ongoing warfare between Ptolemaic south and Seleucid north grinds on through generations. The cycle of revenge and counter-attack mirrors the 'wars and rumors of wars' that Jesus names as the background noise of the present age (Matthew 24:6). He does not promise the end of such cycles before his return; he promises presence and ultimate victory through them.
8: The king of the south carries away Egyptian gods and precious vessels into Egypt and prevails for years. The stripping of the northern kingdom's divine images — a common ancient-world demonstration of the defeated gods' powerlessness — anticipates the NT proclamation that all idols are 'made with hands' and have no power over Christ's people. Colossians 2:15: 'He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, triumphing over them.' The Ptolemaic victory over Seleucid gods is a type for Christ's triumph over all spiritual powers.
9: The king of the north invades the southern kingdom and returns to his own land. The recurring incursion-and-withdrawal pattern of the north-south conflict. The back-and-forth of earthly warfare without resolution contrasts with the decisive 'once for all' character of Christ's victory: Hebrews 9:28 — 'Christ, having been offered once to bear the sins of many, will appear a second time, not to deal with sin but to save those who are eagerly waiting for him.' One decisive coming, not endless cycles of advance and withdrawal.
10: The sons of the north stir up war and one advances as far as the enemy's fortress. The escalating conflict between north and south (Antiochus III's campaigns against Egypt). The 'fortress' (maʿôz) that becomes a target in earthly warfare is the same word used in Daniel 11:39 for the false god of fortresses the Antichrist honors. Christ is 'my rock and my fortress' (Psalms 18:2) — the true stronghold that no earthly campaign can overcome: 'The gates of hell shall not prevail against it' (Matthew 16:18).
11: The king of the south marches out against the northern multitude, the multitude is given into his hand, and his heart is exalted. The temporary victory of the south at the battle of Raphia (217 BCE, Ptolemy IV vs. Antiochus III). Military pride at a single victory — the hubris that regularly precedes disaster in the book of Daniel (cf. Nebuchadnezzar in Daniel 4). Christ's counter-word: 'Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth' (Matthew 5:5) — the opposite disposition from the self-exalting victor.
12: The multitude is cast down; the victor's heart is exalted, yet he does not prevail, because Ptolemy IV wastes the advantage. The failure to consolidate victory through spiritual complacency is a recurring motif. The church that wins a battle and then 'does not prevail' through carelessness is warned throughout the NT: 'Let anyone who thinks that he stands take heed lest he fall' (1 Corinthians 10:12). Christ himself 'endures to the end' (Hebrews 12:2-3) — the model of consistent faithfulness without self-exaltation.
13: The king of the north returns with a multitude greater than the first, including many allies after some years. The patient rebuilding of power by Antiochus III, who eventually defeats Ptolemy V at Paneion (198 BCE). The geopolitical patience of Antiochus III — waiting years and building alliances — models the patient cunning that characterizes adversarial power in the NT too. The church's patience is of a different quality: waiting for Christ's return in active service rather than military buildup.
14: 'Many shall rise against the king of the south, and the violent among your own people shall lift themselves up in order to fulfill the vision, but they shall fail.' The Jewish 'violent ones' (ûbĕnê pārîṣê ʿammĕkā) who collaborate with Antiochus III against Ptolemy, thinking they fulfill the prophetic vision — but they fall. The zealot impulse to force the fulfillment of prophetic vision through human violence is a recurring failure mode. Jesus resists this at every turn: he refuses to be made king by force (John 6:15), rebukes Peter's sword (Matthew 26:52), and enters Jerusalem on a donkey rather than a war horse.
15: The king of the north captures the fortified city; the southern forces cannot stand. Antiochus III's conquest of the strongholds of the Levant. The fortress vocabulary (ʿîr miṣṣārôt, fortified city) and the theme of military inability to stand anticipate Daniel 11:39's 'god of fortresses' — the Antichrist who trusts in military might. Christ's summary: 'Everyone who hears these words of mine and does them will be like a wise man who built his house on the rock' (Matthew 7:24) — the true fortress is his word, not military fortification.
16: 'But he who comes against him shall do as he wills, and none shall stand before him. And he shall stand in the glorious land, with destruction in his hand.' The conqueror stands in the 'glorious land' (haṣṣĕbî) — the covenant land of Israel — with destruction. The beauty of the promised land becomes a military position. Christ's 'standing in the glorious land' is of a wholly different kind: he stands in Jerusalem not as a foreign conqueror but as the fulfillment of all the land's covenantal promises, declaring the year of the LORD's favor (Luke 4:18-21).
17: The northern king plans to enter with the strength of his whole kingdom, and to arrange terms, and shall give him the daughter of women to ruin it, but she shall not stand or be for him. The 'daughter of women' (Cleopatra I given to Ptolemy V) is intended as a political spy but instead supports her husband. Political marriages as instruments of empire — the manipulation of intimacy for power. The NT answer: the bride of Christ (Revelation 19:7-8) is not a political arrangement but a covenant of genuine love, prepared by the Lamb for his own sake, not as an instrument of domination.
18: He turns to the coastlands and captures many, but a commander halts his reproach; the reproach is turned back on him. The Roman legatus Popilius Laenas turns Antiochus III back at Eleusis (168 BCE). The halting of an empire's advance by an unexpected confrontation. The 'reproach' (ḥerpāh) that is turned back on its perpetrator anticipates the great reversal in Christ: Christ who bore reproach (Psalms 22:6; Hebrews 13:13) has all reproach returned on the adversary who cast it — 'he who is in you is greater than he who is in the world' (1 John 4:4).
19: He returns to the fortresses of his own land, stumbles, falls, and is no more. Antiochus III dies in a failed campaign in Elam (187 BCE) — the mighty king who touched the glorious land comes to an ignoble end. The pattern of worldly power: rise, domination, stumble, end. Christ's trajectory is precisely inverted: descent, suffering, death — and then exaltation to the right hand of the Father. 'He humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death ... therefore God has highly exalted him' (Philippians 2:8-9).
20: His successor sends an exactor of tribute through the glorious kingdom, but he shall be broken in neither anger nor battle within a few days. Seleucus IV, who sends Heliodorus to plunder the Jerusalem temple treasury (2 Macc 3) and is then assassinated. The brief, undistinguished reign of one who treats the holy place as a revenue source. The contrast with Christ's relation to the sanctuary is complete: Jesus cleanses the temple of its commercialization (John 2:14-17) rather than exploiting it for revenue, embodying what the temple was always meant to be.
21: 'In his place shall arise a contemptible person to whom royal honor has not been given. He shall come in without warning and obtain the kingdom by flatteries.' Antiochus IV Epiphanes — the 'contemptible person' (nibzeh) who seizes power through intrigue rather than legitimate succession. He is the primary Antichrist type in the OT. The contrast with Christ is exact: Jesus receives his kingdom not by flattery but by the Father's eternal decree ('You are my Son; today I have begotten you,' Psalms 2:7) and by the completed work of redemption ('worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power,' Revelation 5:12).
22: 'Armies shall be utterly swept away before him and broken, even the prince of the covenant.' The 'prince of the covenant' (nĕśî bĕrît) swept away is the high priest Onias III, murdered in 171 BCE — the legitimate covenantal office holder destroyed by the contemptible person. The murder of the legitimate high priest by the Antichrist-type foreshadows the execution of Jesus, the true high priest. But Christ cannot be permanently 'swept away': Hebrews 7:24 — 'he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever.'
23: 'And from the time that an alliance is made with him he shall act deceitfully, and he shall become strong with a small people.' Antiochus IV's duplicitous rise — working through deception with a small base. The vocabulary of deception (mirmāh, deceit) is the characteristic mark of the adversarial power in apocalyptic literature. Satan is 'the father of lies' (John 8:44); the Antichrist in 2 Thessalonians 2:9 comes 'with all wicked deception.' Christ's contrast: 'No deceit was found in his mouth' (1 Peter 2:22, quoting Isaiah 53:9) — the truth-teller against the deceiver.
24: He enters the richest districts of the province and does what his fathers never did — scattering plunder, spoil, and goods among his followers to buy loyalty. The corruption of patronage — distributing stolen wealth to secure political allegiance. The NT critique of this worldly pattern: 'You know that the rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them, and their great ones exercise authority over them. It shall not be so among you' (Matthew 20:25-26). Christ distributes not plunder but himself — his body and blood given for many.
25: He stirs up his power against the king of the south with a great army. The first campaign of Antiochus IV against Egypt (169 BCE). Military buildup and the cultivation of power — the posture of worldly kingdoms. The NT diagnosis: 'What causes quarrels and what causes fights among you? Is it not this, that your passions are at war within you?' (James 4:1). The wars between north and south begin in the hearts of those who love domination; Christ's kingdom begins in the heart's surrender.
26: Those who eat his food shall break him; his army is swept away and many fall down slain. Treachery from Antiochus IV's own advisors. The betrayal-from-within pattern — the one fed at the king's table who destroys him — has a terrible inversion in the Passion narrative: Judas, who ate at Jesus's table (John 13:18: 'he who ate my bread has lifted his heel against me,' citing Psalms 41:9), betrays him. But in Christ's case the betrayal becomes the instrument of redemption, not destruction.
27: 'And as for the two kings, their hearts shall be bent on doing evil. They shall speak lies at the same table, but to no avail, for the end is yet to be at the time appointed.' Both kings deceive each other at the conference table — the diplomatic summit as theater of mutual deception. The 'appointed time' (lammôʿēd) governs even the deceptions of kings: God's timetable overrides human scheming. Christ declares: 'the hour is coming, and is now here' (John 5:25) — the appointed time is not merely governing the kings of the north and south but the hour of the Son of Man's glorification (John 12:23).
28: 'And he shall return to his land with great wealth, but his heart shall be set against the holy covenant. And he shall work his will and return to his own land.' The 'holy covenant' (bĕrît qōdeš) against which Antiochus IV sets his heart — the Mosaic covenant and its cultic practices. Christ is the fulfillment and guarantor of the holy covenant: 'This cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood' (Luke 22:20). What Antiochus opposes, Christ seals; what Antiochus defiles, Christ cleanses; what Antiochus destroys, Christ restores and surpasses.
29: At the appointed time he returns south, but this time it shall not be as before. The second campaign against Egypt is cut short by Rome's intervention (168 BCE, the 'Day of Eleusis'). The 'appointed time' (lammôʿēd) is YHWH's calendar, not Antiochus's. The same principle governs Christ's ministry: 'My hour has not yet come' (John 2:4; Daniel 7:30; Daniel 8:20) until 'Father, the hour has come' (John 17:1). The divine appointment of times is the backbone of redemptive history.
30: Ships of Kittim come against him, and he is discouraged and returns; he is furious against the holy covenant and takes action. Antiochus IV, humiliated by Rome, turns his fury against the Jews. The pattern: imperial humiliation redirected as violence against the covenant people. The church is warned of the same misdirected fury: 'the devil has come down to you in great wrath, because he knows that his time is short' (Revelation 12:12). The short time of the adversary's rage is bounded by the same divine appointment that bounded Antiochus's campaigns.
31: 'Forces from him shall appear and profane the temple and fortress, and shall take away the regular burnt offering. And they shall set up the abomination that makes desolate.' The desecration of the temple in 167 BCE — the abolition of the tamid and the erection of an idol on the altar. Jesus cites this very verse in Mark 13:14: 'when you see the abomination of desolation spoken of by the prophet Daniel, standing where he ought not to be ... then let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains.' The Antiochean desecration becomes the type for the end-time desecration that precedes Christ's return. The abomination that makes desolate is answered by the one who makes holy: Christ, the true temple, whose body was destroyed and raised (John 2:19).
32: 'He shall seduce with flattery those who violate the covenant, but the people who know their God shall stand firm and take action.' The key distinction: those who 'know their God' (yōdĕʿê ʾĕlōhāyw) stand firm against the seduction of flattery. Jesus distinguishes his sheep from the rest by knowledge: 'I know my own and my own know me' (John 10:14). The mutual knowledge between shepherd and sheep is the same 'knowing God' that enables the Maccabean faithful to resist. The NT term for the persevering saints — those who 'endure to the end' (Matthew 24:13) — is the equivalent community.
33: 'And the wise among the people shall make many understand, though for some days they shall stumble by sword and flame, by captivity and plunder.' The teachers who instruct the many (maśkîlê ʿam) — the Hasidim or early Pharisees — suffer martyrdom. The paradigm of teaching-through-suffering: Jesus himself is 'the way, the truth, and the life' (John 14:6) whose own suffering is the supreme teaching. Hebrews 11 catalogues the cloud of witnesses who instructed through their endurance; Christ is the 'founder and perfecter of our faith' whose cross is the definitive teaching (Hebrews 12:2).
34: 'When they stumble, they shall receive a little help. And many shall join themselves to them with flattery.' The little help in time of persecution — possibly the Maccabees — is partial, and is contaminated by insincere adherents. The church age is similarly characterized by incomplete help and mixed membership. Jesus's parable of the wheat and tares (Matthew 13:24-30) addresses exactly this: insincere joiners mingle with genuine disciples until the harvest. The 'little help' points forward to the complete help that comes only at the appearing of the Son of Man.
35: 'And some of the wise shall stumble, so that they may be refined, purified, and made white, until the time of the end, for it still awaits the appointed time.' The suffering of the wise has a purifying purpose — 'liṣrôp ûlĕbarrēr ûlĕlabbēn' (to refine, to purify, to make white). Revelation 7:14 uses this very language: 'They have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb.' The refinement Daniel foresees for the Maccabean period is the same refinement the church undergoes — with the crucial difference that the whitening agent is not suffering alone but the blood of Christ applied through suffering.
36: 'And the king shall do as he wills. He shall exalt himself and magnify himself above every god, and shall speak astonishing things against the God of gods. He shall prosper till the indignation is accomplished; for what is decreed shall be done.' The self-exalting king who magnifies himself above every god — the template for the ultimate adversary. Paul draws directly from this verse in 2 Thessalonians 2:4: the man of lawlessness 'opposes and exalts himself against every so-called god or object of worship, so that he takes his seat in the temple of God, proclaiming himself to be God.' Christ is the anti-type: Philippians 2:6 — though he 'was in the form of God, he did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped' — the one who truly is God and refuses to grasp it, against the one who is not God and grasps it.
37: 'He shall pay no attention to the gods of his fathers, or to the one beloved by women, nor pay attention to any other god, for he shall magnify himself above all.' The negation of all prior religious loyalty — the self-exalting king worships only himself. The NT Antichrist is similarly characterized by absolute self-reference: 'he takes his seat in the temple of God, proclaiming himself to be God' (2 Thessalonians 2:4). The absence of the 'one beloved by women' (possibly the fertility deity Tammuz, or the Messiah of Jewish hope) is consistent with an end-time figure who rejects the Messianic claim.
38: 'He shall honor the god of fortresses instead of these. A god whom his fathers did not know he shall honor with gold and silver, with precious stones and costly gifts.' The 'god of fortresses' (ʾĕlôah maʿûzzîm) — military power deified. The ultimate Antichrist worships strength itself. The contrast with Christ is complete: 'My kingdom is not of this world. If my kingdom were of this world, my servants would have been fighting' (John 18:36). Christ refuses the military fortresses of worldly kingship; the Antichrist makes them his god.
39: He deals with the strongest fortresses, honors those who acknowledge him, and divides the land for a price. The distribution of conquered territory as political patronage — the land itself treated as a commodity. The land of Israel as currency for the self-exalting king contrasts with the NT eschatology of inheritance: 'Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth' (Matthew 5:5). The land that the Antichrist sells, Christ gives as inheritance to the meek who receive it from him as gift, not conquest.
40: 'At the time of the end, the king of the south shall attack him, and the king of the north shall rush against him like a whirlwind, with chariots and horsemen and with many ships.' The final military campaign of the 'time of the end' (ʿēt qēṣ). The warfare of the end-time — chariots, horsemen, ships — is the escalating violence that precedes the Parousia. Revelation 6:1-8 catalogues the same spectrum of end-time violence (conquest, war, famine, death) that precedes Christ's appearing. The gathering of the nations for war is the penultimate moment before the ultimate resolution.
41: He enters the glorious land and many countries shall be overthrown, but Edom, Moab, and the main part of the Ammonites shall escape his hand. The selective destruction at the end of history — some nations escape; others do not. The motif of the 'glorious land' invaded and violated reaches its final iteration here: the land that belongs to YHWH's covenant people is the focal point of the last great assault. Christ's Parousia is the answer to this final assault — Zechariah 14:2-4: 'all the nations will be gathered against Jerusalem ... and the LORD will go out and fight against those nations.'
42: He stretches his hand against the countries, and Egypt shall not escape. The extension of the adversarial power's reach across the nations — no human power can provide sanctuary. The NT parallel: in the tribulation period, those without the mark cannot buy or sell (Revelation 13:17). The apparent omnipotence of the end-time adversary — 'who is like the beast, and who can fight against it?' (Revelation 13:4) — is the darkness before the dawn. Christ's return is the reversal of this apparent omnipotence.
43: He shall rule over the treasures of gold and silver and all the precious things of Egypt, and Libya and Cush shall follow in his train. The end-time king controls the wealth of the nations. Revelation 18 describes the same concentration of global wealth in Babylon the Great, whose fall is mourned by the merchants of the earth. The redistribution of wealth that Christ accomplishes is the antithesis of Babylon's hoarding: the new Jerusalem has gates always open, and the nations bring their glory into it (Revelation 21:25-26) — free access rather than controlled accumulation.
44: News from east and north shall alarm him, and he shall go out with great fury to destroy and devote many to destruction. The adversarial king's final campaign — driven by alarm rather than strategy, by fury rather than purpose. The contrast with Christ's Second Coming could not be greater: Christ returns not in alarm but in certainty, not in fury but in righteous judgment, not to destroy recklessly but to 'destroy the destroyer of the earth' (Revelation 11:18) and to save those who are eagerly waiting for him (Hebrews 9:28).
45: 'And he shall pitch his palatial tents between the sea and the glorious holy mountain. Yet he shall come to his end, with none to help him.' The adversary's final camp pitched between the Mediterranean and the 'glorious holy mountain' — between sea and Jerusalem. He comes to his end — wĕʾên ʿōzēr lô (with none to help him) — the final isolation of the adversarial power. Revelation 19:20: the beast 'was captured ... and thrown alive into the lake of fire.' The 'none to help' echoes Christ's own desolation at the cross (Psalms 22:11) but with opposite meaning: Christ's 'none to help' is redemptive; the adversary's is final judgment. On the holy mountain where the adversary camps, Christ will return to reign (Zechariah 14:4; Acts 1:11-12).
